text: The surgeon lifts the mediastinal pleura to expose and identify the right vagus nerve in the infra-azygous compartment. The azygous vein comes into view as the dissection proceeds cranially. The branch of the vagus nerve to the right main bronchus is identified and preserved.
The surgeon identifies and holds the azygos vein, dissecting around it. Hem-o-lok clips are applied on both sides of the vein to secure it, followed by cutting the vein with laparoscopic scissors.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right recurrent laryngeal nerve by first identifying and pulling the right vagus nerve. The dissection proceeds cranially in the suprazygous portion, cutting the first layer of the mediastinal pleura while ensuring the vagus nerve remains intact beneath. The dissection continues up to the right subclavian artery, where the right recurrent laryngeal nerve branches off.

The surgeon lifts the mediastinal pleura and incises it up to the thoracic inlet, carefully cutting the outer first layer. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visualized hooking around the right subclavian artery, while fibro fatty tissue, lymphatics, and lymph nodal basins are lifted into the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the right recurrent laryngeal nerve, avoiding high-energy sources to prevent contact with the nerve. Scissors are used to lift the dissected tissue, ensuring lymphatic and fatty tissue is included in the specimen. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible beneath the scissors, with a small nerve twig supplying the trachea.

The surgeon carefully dissects the proximal vagus nerve from the surrounding lymph nodal basin structures, ensuring preservation of the main branch that innervates the vocal cords. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visibly hooked around the pulsating right subclavian artery.

The surgeon uses a combination of energy sources, scissors, and ligature to safely dissect the recurrent laryngeal nerve from the trachea. Fibro fatty tissue and lymphatic tissue are lifted off the recurrent laryngeal nerve from the lateral border of the trachea, pushing the nerve down as lymphatics and adhesions are released.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the recurrent laryngeal nerve, carefully cutting small nerve twigs that extend to the trachea and esophagus. The dissection proceeds along the lateral border of the trachea and the medial border of the recurrent laryngeal nerve towards the thoracic inlet. The entire course of the recurrent laryngeal nerve, from the subclavian artery to the thoracic inlet, is visible, with the nerve and its branches pushed down away from the esophagus and trachea. The inferior thyroid artery is identified, allowing for the safe use of an energy source.

The surgeon uses an energy source to manage vessels and veins that may disrupt the dissection field. The fibro fatty tissue and lymph nodal basins are lifted off from the lateral wall of the trachea and the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The dissection is complete, revealing the right inferior thyroid artery in close proximity to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

That brings us to the five easy steps of how a recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection should be done. At the end of the procedure, it is important to have hemostasis. We usually use Evacil to bring hemostasis for small bleeding points, which are sprayed on at the end of the procedure. Let's revise the anatomy. That is the recurrent arangeal nerve taking off from the right vagus. The right recurrent arangeal nerve goes all above, all along the lateral border of the trachea. Here it hooks around the subclavian artery and goes cranially towards the neck to get inserted into the vocal cords. So right recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection is also called as suprazygous mediastinal lymphadenectomy. It is also called as extended two-field lymphadenectomy. It is important to know that any thoracoscopic esophagectomy is a team effort and we from Max Institute of Cancer Care Delhi bring this video to you. Thank you.
